content:
# 2 Corinthians Chapter 2 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2CO_002_page_001.jpg

### The Painful Visit and the Tearful Letter

1. So I made up my mind that I would not make another painful visit to you.

2. For if I cause you pain, who will be left to make me glad? Only you, the very ones I have grieved.

3. That is why I wrote to you as I did, so that when I do come, I won't be grieved by the very ones who ought to give me the greatest joy. I am confident that my joy is shared by all of you.

4. I wrote that letter in great anguish, with a troubled heart and many tears. I didn't write it to hurt you but to let you know how much I love you.

### Forgiveness for the Offender

5. I am not overstating it when I say that the man who caused all the trouble hurt all of you more than he hurt me.

6. Most of you opposed him, and that was punishment enough.

7. Now, however, it is time to forgive and comfort him. Otherwise he may be overcome by discouragement.

8. So I urge you now to reaffirm your love for him.

9. I wrote to you as I did to test you and see if you would fully comply with my instructions.

10. When you forgive this man, I forgive him too. And when I forgive whatever needs to be forgiven, I do so with Christ's authority for your benefit,

11. so that Satan will not outsmart us. For we are familiar with his evil schemes.

### Anxiety in Troas

12. When I came to the city of Troas to preach the Good News of Christ, the Lord opened a door of opportunity for me.

13. But I had no peace of mind because my dear brother Titus hadn't arrived yet. So I said good-bye and went on to Macedonia to find him.

### The Triumphal Procession

14. But thank God! He has made us his captives and continues to lead us along in Christ's triumphal procession. Now he uses us to spread the knowledge of Christ everywhere, like a sweet perfume.

15. Our lives are a Christ-like fragrance rising up to God. But this fragrance is perceived differently by those who are being saved and by those who are perishing.

16. To those who are perishing, we are a dreadful smell of death and doom. But to those who are being saved, we are a life-giving perfume. And who is adequate for such a task as this?

17. You see, we are not like the many hucksters who preach for personal profit. We preach the word of God with sincerity and with Christ's authority, knowing that God is watching.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verses 1-4 — The tearful letter:** Paul is not referring to 1 Corinthians here. Between 1 and 2 Corinthians, Paul made a disastrous visit to Corinth (the "painful visit") and then wrote an anguished letter (the "tearful letter," now lost). Someone in Corinth personally attacked Paul during that visit, and the church had to decide whose side they were on. This is the background to everything in 2 Corinthians.

**Verses 5-11 — Forgive him:** The church disciplined the offender — good. But now Paul says: enough. Stop punishing him and start loving him again. Excessive punishment serves Satan's purposes, not God's. The goal of church discipline is always restoration, never destruction. Paul's pastoral wisdom here is remarkable: he balances justice with mercy, community standards with individual compassion.

**Verses 12-13 — "I had no peace of mind":** Even though God opened a door for ministry in Troas, Paul couldn't take advantage of it because he was so worried about the Corinthians. He left a fruitful ministry opportunity because his love for this church was overwhelming his ability to focus. This is not weakness — this is what love looks like in a pastor.

**Verses 14-17 — Christ's victory parade:** Paul reaches for one of his most vivid metaphors. In a Roman triumphal procession, the victorious general paraded through the streets, with incense burning along the route. Paul sees himself not as the general but as a captive led in Christ's parade. And the gospel he carries is like that incense — it smells like life to some and death to others. The same message, the same fragrance, but the response depends on the hearer. And the question "who is adequate?" is not rhetorical despair — it's the setup for Paul's answer in chapter 3: our adequacy comes from God.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
